artifacts.description: A typed change contract containing scope, claims, criteria, and definition of done.
Canonical plan.json and manifest.json generated from the contract.
An independent review attached to the proposed change.

assumptions.statement: The initial contract is accepted directly by the project owner before external review enforcement exists.
The organization currently has one human maintainer available to approve governance changes.

decisions.title: Development governance is a deterministic contract
Signed identity is enforced at the GitHub trust boundary
Product specifications are deterministic contract inputs
OverlayKit is a secure show-centered production studio
Production intents name their target and direct Program authority
Production cues compose intents with explicit partial outcomes
Control feedback separates server truth from delivery acknowledgement
Feedback admission binds signed provenance to device authority
Device credential generations bind exact authority
Device credential persistence is single-instance atomic
Device bearers use versioned domain-separated verifiers
Protocol publishes one compiled ESM implementation
Server composes device authority before network listen
Device control derives exact authority from route coordinates
Action catalogs project capability and authority intersection
Device action catalogs are authenticated point-in-time discovery
Component visibility feedback derives only from server-known structure

gates.description: Typed change contracts satisfy their schema and epistemic invariants.
The deterministic governance host validates and verifies the stored plan and manifest.
A reviewer other than the last producer approves the change.
Lint, type-check, and automated tests pass.
The workflow commit and every pull-request commit carry an identity signature verified by the GitHub root of trust.

mechanisms.expectedCommand: npm run governance:observe:github -- --run artifacts/governance-run.json --out artifacts/github-evidence.json --observation-out artifacts/github-observation.json
mechanisms.subject: github-root-of-trust-evidence

rules.statement: An accepted decision is never edited or deleted; a successor decision derives supersession.
An accepted product specification is never edited or deleted; a successor specification derives supersession.

Passing pure projection tests may not be presented as HTTP publication, server inventory authority, saved-Scene discovery, Take, value, trigger or cue support, feedback, Studio parity, Companion integration, hardware behavior, or execution authorization.
The complete action inventory must be bounded, supported, canonical, and unique before projection; malformed inventory cannot yield a partial catalog.
A catalog descriptor may describe an action but must never grant command execution; every command still requires fresh authorization at its execution boundary.
Catalog descriptors must omit bearer and credential material, interactive identity, granted scope lists, production revisions, and assertions of current state.
Equivalent inventory and authority must produce byte-equivalent catalog output using fixed semantic ranks and locale-independent text ordering without mutating inputs.
An action may be projected only when its Show, required scope, exact Preview or Program target, canonical control identifier, and explicit inventory capability all intersect.
An agent identity has no independent project authority and must name its human principal.
Version-two architecture, product, security, and documentation changes reference the specifications whose observable criteria authorize their scope.
Every change separates facts, inferences, assumptions, and unknowns; facts and inferences require evidence.
Studio sessions, OBS output tokens, and device tokens are separate, revocable credentials with least-privilege scopes.
One cue operation identifier is idempotent only for identical cue content and expected initial revision.
Cue steps execute in declared order and execution stops at the first failed step.
A cue names one Show and one Preview or Program target that applies to every contained intent.
Every cue preserves ADR-0005 target revision checks, direct Program authorization, runtime-only mutation, and honest receipt scope.
One production action is a one-step cue and sequential coordinated work uses the same cue contract with additional ordered steps.

Every device action must resolve and authorize the bearer against the exact route-derived authority before Show lookup or production mutation so rotation, expiration, revocation, and scope changes fail closed on the next request.
Device authorization requires the credential's exact Show, declared scope, allowed Preview or Program target, and allowed control identifier.
Every device action and feedback-authority projection resolves the presented bearer against current expiration, revocation, generation, and sealed-secret state before admission.
Production REST and WebSocket listeners may be created only after protocol loading, device credential store initialization, and lifecycle composition succeed.
Only successful issue and rotate responses may disclose a device bearer, those responses must be non-cacheable, API responses must omit sealedSecret, and persistence must omit bearer plaintext.
Issuance and rotation return an opaque bearer once while credential storage receives only its sealed representation.
Device control accepts exactly one Bearer credential from Authorization and must reject credential transport through query parameters, request bodies, cookies, or duplicate Authorization fields.

The inventory adapter must stop before exceeding 1,000 aggregate capabilities, bound labels to protocol limits, and allow duplicate target-control identities to fail the complete pure projection rather than silently choosing one.
Every device catalog request must freshly authenticate exactly one bearer and must not accept cookies, query authority, duplicate Authorization fields, or a Studio session as device authority.
The authenticated device authority must name the exact route-derived Show, and inventory may be read only after that Show is confirmed to exist and remain active.
Selecting a catalog entry never bypasses fresh command authorization, active-Show checks, expected revision, idempotency, or domain validation at execution time.

A CommonJS server must load the ESM catalog projector through native asynchronous import behind an injected port and must verify the compiled runtime rather than synchronously requiring the ESM package.

Passing this HTTP slice may not be presented as feedback delivery, expected-revision delivery, catalog version coherence, distributed atomicity, saved-Scene discovery, Take, value, trigger or cue support, Studio parity, Companion integration, or hardware behavior.
Visibility inventory is derived from immutable copies of currently loaded Preview and Program element trees, including nested components and excluding unloaded targets.
HTTP catalog responses must be marked no-store and must not include current value, production revision, credential material, or any claim that a descriptor authorizes future execution.

Passing local device HTTP tests may not be presented as proof of network delivery, renderer or OBS application, device feedback, catalogs, cues, Take, value mutation, Companion or hardware support, device-specific rate limits, audit retention, multi-process coordination, or in-flight revocation or Show-archival linearizability.
Device visibility execution must preserve ProductionService expected-revision, operation-id replay, operation-id conflict, Preview isolation, and bounded server-state receipt semantics.
A device Control API request must resolve only device authority and must never authenticate, construct, or inherit a Studio session principal.
A mutation writes and synchronizes a private unique temporary document, atomically replaces the prior complete file, and advances memory only after that replacement succeeds.
Exactly one credential-store instance may own and mutate a device credential file within the supported self-hosted runtime boundary.
The credential file accepts only sealed secret material and rejects a raw OverlayKit device bearer before persistence.
Credential mutations are serialized within the owning store instance and replacements commit only when the persisted generation equals the caller's expected generation.
Restart persistence and atomic visibility proven for one owning store instance may not be presented as multi-instance, multi-process, distributed, backup, or power-loss durability.
The complete versioned credential document and its lifecycle invariants must validate before any persisted record is exposed or mutated.

Passing adapter tests may not be presented as primitive certification, memory or host compromise resistance, transport security, bearer recovery, replay prevention, rate limiting, endpoint integration, or hardware support.
Entropy or sealing failure prevents credential persistence, and verifier parsing, digest, or comparison failure denies authentication.
ProductionService may receive direct Program authorization from the device route only after the same request bearer has been freshly authorized for the exact Program target and control.
Credential lifecycle accepts Owner identity, clock, entropy, secret sealing, and storage authority only from trusted adapters outside the bearer credential.
Device credential issue, rotate, and revoke operations require an authenticated Owner session and the interactive browser-origin boundary.
Issue authority comes from an existing active Show route, and rotate or revoke must reject a stored credential that does not belong to the active Show named by that route.
A valid device bearer resolves to device authority and never to a Studio user, role set, cookie, or interactive session.
Rotation preserves credential identity, replaces its secret, increments generation, and binds feedback audience to credentialId.generation so prior generations fail closed.
A production device secret must be generated from exactly 32 bytes returned by the approved cryptographic random source and encoded as canonical unpadded base64url.

Passing bootstrap and Owner HTTP tests may not be presented as credential listing, response-loss recovery, concurrent archival linearizability, device request authentication, command or feedback transport, UI or Companion integration, registry provenance, multi-process coordination, in-flight revocation, rate-limit policy, audit logging, or hardware support.
The device verifier construction applies only to a 256-bit machine-generated secret and may not replace the approved KDF for human passwords.
A stored device verifier must use the accepted version and algorithm and hash the complete bearer after the device-credential domain separator.
Authentication must reject unsupported or noncanonical verifier representations before comparing an equal-length 32-byte candidate and stored digest with the approved constant-time primitive.

The device visibility route must derive Show, Preview or Program target, and canonical `<componentId>.visibility` control authority from route coordinates and must fix the required scope to component.visibility:write.
A device visibility body may contain only visible, operationId, and expectedRevision and must not name or override Show, scope, target, component, control, role, or direct-Program authority.
A change is ready only when every enforced gate passed and every enforced artifact is present for the current planHash and manifestHash.
A governance run may report outcomes only for gates and bindings already present in its matching plan and manifest.
Credential audience and feedback scopes come from externally established authority and may never be granted by fields inside the feedback envelope.
Trusted feedback signs a canonical envelope containing its schema version, issuer key, credential audience, sequence, and complete normalized event.
An explicit operation failure remains failed until a strictly later authoritative-server observation recovers the control.
Feedback admission verifies the signature against an issuer key pinned outside the received envelope.
A server observation is current for less than three seconds and becomes stale with unknown button state at exactly three seconds.
A feedback sequence at or below the last admitted sequence is replay and cannot reach the projector.
A signed event is admitted only with feedback-read scope for its exact Show, Preview or Program target, and control identifier.
Control feedback reports authoritative server truth only and cannot be presented as renderer or hardware observation without separate evidence.
Every control feedback projection names one Show, one Preview or Program target, and one control identifier; feedback for another subject fails closed.
Hardware and third-party controller support integrates through the vendor-neutral Control API and never defines the core production command model.
Normative governance is the compiled plan derived from accepted immutable decisions, accepted immutable specifications, and the active profile.
One production bootstrap invocation must share one initialized device credential store and its lifecycle across all mounted Owner lifecycle routes.
Only bounded normalized fields covered by the signature may enter the trusted feedback projector; extra fields are discarded.
A component exposes operator capabilities through typed control definitions rather than inferred variables or arbitrary implementation access.
A failed cue reports every completed step, the failed step and error, and the resulting revision without claiming rollback or atomicity.
The default production workflow prepares Preview and promotes it to Program through an explicit Take command.
Every production intent names Preview or Program and a missing or unknown target fails closed.
Direct Program authority is established by an authorization boundary and is never asserted by the intent payload itself.
A CommonJS consumer that loads protocol runtime values must use native dynamic import; synchronous require and a separate CommonJS implementation are outside the accepted contract.
Every supported protocol entry point must declare explicit types and import targets, and every target must be present in the publication payload.

Passing tarball-consumer tests may not be presented as registry publication, provenance, semantic-version compatibility, browser or bundler compatibility, mixed loader identity, synchronous require support, server composition, endpoint integration, or complete downstream compatibility.
Publication-boundary evidence must exercise an installed npm tarball and may not substitute direct workspace-source imports for packed-consumer behavior.
The protocol publication allowlist must expose compiled dist artifacts rather than repository source or tests.
The public protocol runtime must be compiled from its TypeScript source as one ESM implementation accompanied by declarations.
A server receipt reports resulting authoritative server state and cannot be presented as renderer or device observation without additional evidence.
Preview and Program state never overwrite a saved Scene or Component Definition without an explicit save-back command.
Replaying one operation identifier with the same intent does not apply twice, and reuse for another intent fails as a conflict.
A production intent changes runtime state only and never overwrites saved Scene source.
Only a revisioned authoritative-server observation may establish current active or inactive control state.
The CommonJS server must load the ESM protocol runtime through native dynamic import, and its production build must reject a rewrite of that package boundary to synchronous require.
Every saved scene, production session, output, and show-local control belongs to an explicit Show.
Authoring, production, library, security, and integrations share one authenticated Studio shell; the headless OBS output remains separate.
A production intent validates the expected revision of its named target before mutation.
Transport acknowledgement is delivery metadata and may never establish, refresh, or recover production state.
A component visibility capability uses the canonical control identifier `<componentId>.visibility` in catalog and authorization subjects.
Each projected visibility observation must bind the snapshot Show, exact Preview or Program target, canonical catalog control subject, snapshot revision, and explicit observation time.
A supplied action catalog selects eligible subjects but the visibility projector does not establish catalog freshness, future execution authority, or coherence with later production revisions.

Opacity, visibility, classes, clipping, transforms, computed style, renderer pixels, OBS state, delivery, and hardware state may not alter or be claimed by server-known structural visibility projection.
A component may be reported active only when its own known inline display state and that of every ancestor are not display none after whitespace and case normalization.

Passing the pure projection may not be presented as trusted provenance, clock authority, signed or authenticated delivery, three-second scheduling, acknowledgement, operation failure, reconnect recovery, renderer observation, Studio or Companion consumption, or hardware behavior.

Projection must reject Show mismatch, malformed relied-upon structure, malformed descriptors, duplicate subjects or component identifiers, missing addressed components, invalid revisions or times, observation before a known snapshot update, and inputs exceeding 1,000 elements or actions without emitting partial output.
Equivalent valid inputs must produce canonically ordered equivalent observations without mutating the snapshot or catalog.

specifications.title: Secure show authoring and live production
